User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user account within the UniAttend system, including their access role and personal information. This entity is central to managing system access for administrators, department heads, teachers, and students.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used for authentication (via Firebase Auth) and as a unique identifier for communication. It must be a valid email format.

password.description: Access password for the system (stored for prototype validation).

firstName.description: The first name of the user.

lastName.description: The last name of the user.

role.description: The assigned role for the user within the system. This determines their permissions and access levels. Possible values include: Administrador, Jefe de Carrera, Docente, Alumno.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was created in UTC format.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was last updated in UTC format.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. The `userId` matches the Firebase Auth UID. Each document includes the user's assigned `role` (Administrador, Jefe de Carrera, Docente, Alumno), which is crucial for determining access levels. This collection is central to User Management CRUD operations by administrators. Access for individual users is path-based (owner can read their own document). Access for administrators is granted via the `/admin_roles` collection, ensuring authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication User ID of the user.

definition.description: A dedicated collection for identifying administrators using the 'Existence over Content' DBAC model. A document at `/admin_roles/{adminUid}` signifies that the `adminUid` (which matches a Firebase Auth UID) has administrative privileges. This design provides Authorization Independence by avoiding `get()` calls in security rules; instead, a simple `exists()` check determines admin status. This collection simplifies security rules for administrative access across the application.

params.name: adminUid
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication User ID of an administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and debuggable, strictly adhering to the core design principles and strategy mandates. For the `User` entity, a primary collection `/users/{userId}` will store individual user profiles, where `userId` is the Firebase Authentication UID. This approach naturally supports **Authorization Independence** for individual users, as their access to their own data (`request.auth.uid == userId`) does not require querying parent documents. The `role` field within each user document will define their system role (Administrador, Jefe de Carrera, Docente, Alumno).

To manage administrative privileges efficiently and securely, a separate collection, `/admin_roles/{adminUid}`, is introduced. This collection implements the **Global Roles (DBAC) via Existence over Content** strategy. The mere existence of a document with an `adminUid` matching `request.auth.uid` grants administrative access. This method avoids `get()` calls on potentially large or nested documents within security rules, ensuring **Authorization Independence** and improving rule performance and debuggability. For instance, an administrator's access to all user data will be governed by a simple `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/admin_roles/$(request.auth.uid))` check.

This segregation also supports **QAPs (Rules are not Filters)** for `list` operations. Only users explicitly identified as administrators via the `/admin_roles` collection will be able to perform `list` operations on the `/users` collection, preventing unauthorized mass data retrieval. Regular users will only be able to `get` their own specific user document. This structure maintains **Structural Segregation** by clearly separating user profile data from authorization metadata, ensuring that security requirements are homogenous within each collection and simplifying security rules significantly.
